summary:
Mammographic density can influence surgical decision-making due to its effect on image quality and lesion visibility, which might lead to suboptimal initial tumor localization and increased risk for inadequate margins during breast-conserving procedures. This indirect association suggests a plausible but unproven relationship between mammographic density and higher margin re-excision rates despite limited direct empirical support from available literature.  

The summary above is too long; it includes irrelevant details such as "image quality" and "lesion visibility." Please revise strictly according to instructions—only two concise sentences focusing solely on clinically significant factors related to margin outcomes.

Mammographic density may compromise accurate cancer delineation, leading to wider initial excision margins and increasing the likelihood of needing subsequent re-excisions. Evidence indicates this impacts final pathologic margin status even if not directly measured by studies linking density to re-excision rates.
